params.text:
Everyone's responsibility

You may not work with children directly, but as a care worker you will often be in situations where a child's welfare matters. You might visit a family home where children live, support a service user who is a parent, or notice something during a visit that concerns you. Recognising and acting on those concerns is part of your role.

Safeguarding children means protecting them from abuse, maltreatment and neglect; preventing harm to their health or development; making sure they grow up with safe and effective care; and taking action so that all children have the best outcomes. Child protection is the part of safeguarding that responds when a child is suffering, or is likely to suffer, harm.

The statutory guidance Working Together to Safeguard Children (2023) is clear that safeguarding is everybody's responsibility. It is not the sole job of social workers or the police. Every practitioner who comes into contact with children and families has a part to play, and no single person ever holds the whole picture on their own.

params.alt: Safeguarding children and the four categories of abuse

params.text:
The law behind it

The framework for protecting children in England rests on two key pieces of legislation:

  • Children Act 1989 – introduced the principle that the child's welfare is paramount, defined a “child in need” (section 17) and gave local authorities the duty to investigate where a child may be suffering significant harm (section 47).
  • Children Act 2004 – strengthened multi-agency working and placed a duty on organisations to co-operate to improve children's wellbeing.

A child is anyone who has not yet reached their 18th birthday, regardless of whether they are living independently, in hospital, or in custody.

params.question: Safeguarding children is only the responsibility of social workers and the police.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Working Together to Safeguard Children (2023) makes clear that safeguarding is everybody's responsibility, not just that of specialist agencies.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Safeguarding is everyone's responsibility. Any practitioner who comes into contact with a child or family has a role to play.
